Facts.text: Otto I the Great is crowned King of Italy
Battle of Blore Heath in Staffordshire, inferior Yorkist force defeats larger Lancaster force in a major battle of the English Wars of the Roses
John I Albert (32) becomes King of Poland, succeeding his father, Casimir IV
Harvard College in Cambridge, Massachusetts, 1st commencement
Jacob Gilles appointed Dutch pension advisor
Conseil of the Cinq-Cents (Council of 500), forms in Paris
Battle of Assaye: British-Indian forces beat Maratha Army
Border demarcation markers for Neutral Moresnet are installed, a small territory administered jointly by the Netherlands and Prussia
Fall of Tripolitsa, Greek forces massacre 30,000 Turks during the Greek War of Independence
Dutch troops occupy Brussels
HMS Beagle sails to Charles Island in Galapagos archipelago
Hunger strike in Hague
The first baseball team, the NY Knickerbockers, organizes and adopts a rulebook
Johann Gottfried Galle & Heinrich d'Arrest find Neptune
Russian warship Leffort disappears in a storm in the Gulf of Finland; 826 die
US President Lincoln's Emancipation Proclamation is published in northern newspapers
Confederate siege of Chattanooga begins
Grito de Lares proclaims Puerto Rico's independence (crushed by Spain)
Tom Allen beats Mike McCale for Heavyweight Boxing title
Ottawa Rough Riders play their 1st game
Hurricane strikes Curacao & Bonaire kills 200
Baldwin steam motors tram 1st tried in Sydney Australia
Richard Rhodes invented a hearing aid called the Audiophone
Jules Ferry forms French government
American Herman Hollerith patents his mechanical tabulating machine, the beginning of data processing
Tongariro National Park formed in New Zealand after proposal by local chief Ngāti Tūwharetoa - fourth national park in the world
Nintendo Koppai (later Nintendo Company, Limited) is founded by Fusajiro Yamauchi to produce and market the playing card game Hanafuda
Ed Cartwright bats in 7 RBIs in 1 inning
Vocational school Troop College, later California Institute of Technology (CalTech) established in Pasadena, California
French labor union CGT (Confederation Generale du Travail) forms
1st frontier days rodeo celebration (Cheyene Wyoming)
British Colonel Francis Younghusband and his expedition with accompanying military force leave Lhasa, Tibet
Mbunga-siege of Fort Mahenge German East-Africa broken
Proclamation sets fineness & weight of silver & bronze coins of Canada
Giant Fred (Bonehead) Merkle fails to touch second, causing the third out in the ninth and disallowing the winning run (game ends tied, Cubs win replay and pennant)
University of Alberta opens
United States Post Office Department designates Earl Ovington "Official Air Mail Pilot #1"
French aviator Roland Garros (24) is 1st to fly over Mediterranean Sea
Serbian troops march into Albania
Women protests take place in the Free State, South Africa, led by Charlotte Maxeke, resisting government attempts to impose passes on women; passes are burnt in front of the municipal offices
Reds drop their 19th straight game, then beat Boston Braves
Alexandre Millerand elected president of France
Despite Japanese protests, China withdraws recognition of Tsarists representatives in China
Berthold Brecht's "Drum in the Night" premieres in Germany
Gdynia Seaport Construction Act passed by the Polish parliament.
80,000 demonstrate in Amsterdam against Fleet law
George Kaufman's "Butter & Egg Man" premieres in NYC
Washington shortstop Roger Peckinpaugh is named AL MVP
MLB New York Yankees commit 5 errors (3 by shortstop Frankie Crosetti), but beat Red Sox 16-12 at Fenway Park, Boston
Yankees lose 9-5 but clinch pennant when Red Sox beat Detroit
Time capsule to be opened in the year 6939 is buried at the World's Fair in NYC; it contains a woman's hat, a man's pipe, and 1,100 feet of microfilm
Cookie Lavagetto goes 6 for 6-Dodgers get 27 hits & beat Phillies 22-4
-27] French/British assault on French fleet in Dakar, flees
General de Gaulle forms government in exile in London
German air raid on Soviet naval base at Kronstadt (battleship Marat sinks)
The first gas murder experiments are conducted at Auschwitz. concentration camp
Soviet counter offensive at Stalingrad
Transport #36 departs with French Jews to nazi-Germany
Beginning of the liquidation of Vilnius' ghettos in Lithuania, an estimated 50,000 people will be killed
Proclamation No. 30 was issued, declaring the existence of a state of war between the Philippines and the United States and the United Kingdom
Braves clinch NL pennant
Canada's first mountain rescue by helicopter of a forestry worker from a Wells Gray Park forestry lookout
US Air Force Mustangs accidentally bomb British on Hill 282 Korea, 17 killed
1st closed circuit pay-TV telecast of a sports event
Brooklyn Dodgers clinch NL pennant with a record of 96-57 over the New York Giants 92-62
KHQA TV channel 7 in Hannibal-Quincy, MO (CBS) begins broadcasting
Pakistan signs Pact of Baghdad
Yanks clinch pennant by beating Red Sox, 3-2
Milwaukee Braves clinch NL pennant by beating Cards 4-2
White mob forces 9 black students enrolled at Little Rock's Central High School in Arkansas to withdraw
WTIC TV channel 61 in Hartford, CT (CBS/FOX) begins broadcasting
Great Britain performs atmospheric nuclear test at Christmas Island
Stravinsky's "Thieni" premieres in Venice
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Australia's first passenger roll on/roll off diesel ferry, the MS Princess of Tasmania makes her maiden voyage across Bass Strait
1st movie to become a TV series, "How to Marry a Millionaire" premieres
NY's Philharmonic Hall (since renamed Avery Fisher Hall) opens as 1st unit of Lincoln Center for Performing Arts
Georgette Ciselet is 1st woman on Belgian Council of State
WMEB TV channel 12 in Orono, ME (PBS) begins broadcasting
Greek Colonels' regime frees ex-Prime Minister Georgios Papandreou
Radio Malta stops testing
WKAS TV channel 25 in Ashland, KY (PBS) begins broadcasting
WKGB TV channel 53 in Bowling Green, KY (PBS) begins broadcasting
WKLE TV channel 46 in Lexington, KY (PBS) begins broadcasting
WKMA TV channel 35 in Madisonville, KY (PBS) begins broadcasting
WKMR TV channel 38 in Morehead, KY (PBS) begins broadcasting
WKON TV channel 52 in Owenton, KY (PBS) begins broadcasting
WKSO TV channel 29 in Somerset, KY (PBS) begins broadcasting
WKZT TV channel 23 in Elizabethtown, KY (PBS) begins broadcasting
1st broadcast of "Marcus Welby MD" on ABC-TV
Abdul Razak bin Hussain becomes premier of Malaysia
Chief Constable of the Royal Ulster Constabulary (RUC) Arthur Young announces his resignation
IBM announces System/370 Model 145, their 1st computer to employ semiconductors for its main memory
2 members of the Official Irish Republican Army are killed in a premature bomb explosion
Largest known prime number, 2 ^ 132,049-1, is found with computer aid by David Slowinski; holds as largest until 1985
BBC Ceefax begins 1st teletext service
Preston Jones' "Texas Trilogy" play premieres in NYC
South Africa decides to allow multi-racial teams to represent them
Soyuz 22 returns to Earth
"I Just Fall in Love Again" single released by The Carpenters
3rd test of Space Shuttle Enterprise
Cheryl Ladd replaces Farrah Fawcett on TV show "Charlie's Angels"
Somali constitution approved by president
Amin Gemayel sworn in as President of Lebanon
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Argentine military regime gives amnesty to military and political assassins
Columbia moves to Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center (Florida), for mating in preparation of STS-9
Gerrie Coetzee of South Africa KOs American Michael Dokes in 10 rounds in Richfield. Ohio for WBA heavyweight boxing title
MLB Philadelphia PhilliesSteve Carltonis 16th to wins 300 game, beating Cardinals 6-2 in St. Louis
36th Emmy Awards: "Hill Street Blues"; "Cheers": John Ritter & Tyne Daly win
Astro Jim Deshaies strikes out 1st 8 Dodger of game (ends with 10)
Congress selects the rose as US national flower
East German swimmer Uwe Dassler sets world record 3:46.95 to win 400m freestyle gold medal at the Seoul Olympics; edges Australian Duncan Armstrong by 0.2s
Fiji's new constitution is proposed
Premiere of director Paul Schrader's crime biopic "Patty Hearst"
Blue Jays bat out of order against Brewers in 6th inning
PBS begins an 11-hour miniseries on the Civil War
Saddam says he will destroy Israel
Bill Comrie purchases BC Lions from CFL
Bradlees announces it will take over Alexander's department stores in NYC
Manon Rheaume becomes the first female to play in an NHL exhibition game when she plays goalie for the Tampa Bay Lightning, allowing 2 goals on 9 attempts in 1 period
Mud storm kills 30 in South France
Paul Chu group reports super conduction at -123°C (record high)
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Wilkes-Barre, Pennsylvania (WZMT 97.9 FM) and in West Palm Beach, Florida (WCLB 95.5 FM)
Seattle Mariners break record for most HRs in a year (258)
NASA announces that it has lost contact with the Mars Climate Orbiter
Qantas Flight 1 overruns the runway in Bangkok during a storm, causing minor injuries to some passengers in what was then the worst crash in the airline's long history
First version of the web browser Mozilla Firefox dubbed Phoenix 0.1 is released to the public
Crime drama "NCIS" starring Mark Harmon, Michael Weatherley, Pauley Perrette and David McCallum premieres on CBS in the US
At least 1,070 people in Haiti are reported killed by floods caused by Hurricane Jeanne
FBI shoots and kills militant and fugitive Filiberto Ojeda Ríos on the outskirts of Hormigueros, Puerto Rico
20 Iranian US Visas are denied, including diplomats and two ministers, ahead of the UN general assembly meeting in New York
Iran blocks the use of Google as a search engine
Scientists discover four genetically distinct types of breast cancer
25 people are killed after Typhoon Usagi passes Hong Kong and China
Women are allowed in King Fahd International Stadium in Riyadh for the first time during Saudi Arabia's 87th anniversary celebrations
Japan's space agency is the first to place two robotic explorers on an asteroid - Ryugu, from its Hayabusa-2 spacecraft
Mud volcano, the Otman-Bozdag, erupts 300m (980ft) high, near Baku, Azerbaijan
178 year old British travel company Thomas Cook goes into liquidation, stranding 600,000 travellers worldwide, prompting the largest postwar repatriation effort by the UK government
Haitian Senator Jean-Marie Ralph Féthière opens fire hitting a journalist leaving parliament in Port-au-Prince
Renewed violence in West Papua region, Indonesia, leaves 27 dead in capital Wamena after protesters set fire to buildings over racism claims
US police officer fired after arresting two six-year-olds at a school on charges of misdemeanour battery in Florida
PresidentDonald Trumprefuses to commit to a peaceful transfer of power after the US November election at a White House press conference
Fossilized footprints 23,000-21,000 years old from White Sands, New Mexico indicate settlement by humans of North and South America earlier than previous thought
Russia holds sham referendums in four partly occupied Ukrainian regions with some Ukrainian citizens voters forced to vote at gunpoint
Israel launches airstrikes against Hezbollah targets in Lebanon killing more than 490 people
Concordat of Worms is agreed between Pope Calixtus II and Holy RomanEmperor Henry V
One of the most extravagant medieval English feasts ever recorded held forRichard IIandJohn of Gauntin London. Included 14 salted oxen, 120 sheep, 1200 pigeons and 11,000 eggs
Battle of Othée; victory ofJohn the Fearless, duke of Burgundy over Liège.
English KingHenry VIIIand Holy RomanEmperor Maximilian Iconquer Tournai, Belgium
KingPhilip II of Spainforbids Spanish settlements in Florida
British Major John Andre revealsBenedict Arnold's plot to betray West Point
Lewis and Clarkreturn to St Louis from Pacific Northwest
Otto von Bismarckis appointed Prime Minister and Foreign Minister of Prussia by KingWilhelm I
Billy the Kidis arrested for the 1st time and jailed after receiving clothing stolen from a Chinese laundry. Escapes two days later.
1stMack Sennett"KeystoneComedy" movie released "Cohen Collects a Debt"

"Upset of the Decade" - Gene Tunney beats defending championJack Dempseyby 10-round unanimous decision at Sesquicentennial Stadium (later known as JFK Stadium), Philadelphia, Pennsylvania for world heavyweight boxing title
"Sunrise: A Song of Two Humans", directed by F. W. Murnau, starring George O'Brien andJanet Gaynor, is released (Academy Awards Unique and Artistic Picture 1927)
AbdulazizIbn Saudmerges the Kingdom of Nejd and Hejaz into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia
British Prime MinisterNeville Chamberlainflies to Munich
Dutch artistPiet Mondrianleaves Europe for New York, where he lives for the rest of his life
Dutch Nazi collaborator Mussert's 1st meeting withAdolf Hitler
First Cavalcade of Jazz outdoor concert held at Wrigley Field, Los Angeles withCount Basie, Valaida Snow and others
MLB Cleveland Indians ownerBill Veeckholds funeral services to bury 1948 pennant
US PresidentHarry Trumanannounces evidence of USSR's 1st nuclear device detonation
Nobel peace prize awarded toRalph Bunchefor mediation in Israel - 1st African American winner
"Kaw-Liga" single recorded byHank Williams
UndefeatedRocky MarcianoKOs defending championJersey Joe Walcottin the 13th round at Municipal Stadium, Philadelphia for the world heavyweight boxing title
US vice-presidential candidateRichard Nixonmakes his "Checkers Speech", defending the gift of a cocker spaniel to his daughters

All male, all white jury finds Roy Brant and John William Milam not guilty of the brutal murder of black teenagerEmmett Tillin Sumner, Mississippi, in landmark case that would help inspire civil rights movement in the US; the two later sell an interview admitting to the murder
"That'll Be the Day" single byBuddy Hollyand The Crickets reaches #1 in the US
ABC's first color TV series "The Jetsons" premieres, created byHanna-Barbera
LA Dodgers shortstopMaury Willsbreaks Ty Cobb's 1915 MLB steals record, recording #97 on his way to 104
Martin Walser's play "Esche und Angora" premieres in Berlin
"Butch Cassidyand the Sundance Kid" directed by George Roy Hill and starringPaul NewmanandRobert Redfordpremieres at the Roger Sherman Theater in New Haven, Connecticut
Northern Star and Illinois Univ newspaper start rumors thatPaul McCartneyis dead
Jan Vermeer's painting "The Love Letter" is stolen from The Centre for Fine Arts, Brussels by a 21-year-old thief
Philippines PresidentFerdinand Marcosannounces state of emergency and declares martial law
Argentine General Elections - formerArgentine President JuanPerón returns to power
John Lennon's single "Whatever Gets You Thru the Night", featuringElton John, released in US; it was Lennon's only solo No. 1 single during his lifetime
US televised presidential debate between Republican incumbentGerald Fordand Democratic challengerJimmy Carter
100,000 cheering Egyptians welcomeAnwar Sadathome from Camp David peace summit
Jane Fonda& 200,000 attend anti-nuke rally in Battery Park, NYC
MLB St Louis Cardinals legendLou Brocksteals 938th & final base of his career
Reggae legendBob Marley's final concert at Stanley Theatre in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
Columbia Records releases "The Nylon Curtain", singer-songwriterBilly Joel's eighth studio album
MLB pitcherGaylord Perry(45) announces his retirement, after 22 seasons; career record 314-265, 3.11 ERA, and 3,534 strikeouts
Detroit TigersSparky Andersonis 1st MLB manager to win 100 games in both leagues
NFL San Francisco 49er quarterbackJoe Montanamisses his 1st start in 49 games
Nigerian PresidentIbrahim Babangidacreates two states: Akwa Ibom and Katisna
Americans Christopher Jacobs, Troy Dalbey, Tom Hunter &Matt Biondiswim world record 3:16.53 to win 4 x 100m freestyle relay at the Seoul Olympics
JoseCansecois baseball's 1st to steal 40 bases & hit 40 HRs
Portuguese world championRosa Motaruns 2:25:40 to score a famous Olympic marathon win over Australian Lisa Martin at the Seoul Games
Jackson Brownereportedly beats girlfriend Daryl Hannah
"The Shawshank Redemption", directed by Frank Darabont and starringTim RobbinsandMorgan Freeman, is released
TV comedy "Modern Family" premieres on ABC, starring Ed O'Neill and Sofia Vergara
64th Emmy Awards: "Homeland", "Modern Family", Damian Lewis &Claire Daneswin
TV series "Mom" premieres created by Chuck Lorre, starringAnna FarisandAllison Janney
Shawn Mendesreleases his album "Illuminate", debuts at No. 1 US Billboard 200
US PresidentDonald Trumpwithdraws invitation to the White House for NBA champions Golden State Warriors afterStephen Currysays he doesn't want to attend

39-year-old New Orleans Saints quarterbackDrew Breesthrows 17 yard pass to Michael Thomas to become NFL career pass leader, breakingBrett Favre's record of 6,300 completions; Saints beat Falcons 43-37
Indian Prime MinisterNarendra Modilaunches "Modicare," a free healthcare program for 500 million people, the world's largest healthcare initiative
Laver Cup Men's Tennis, Chicago:Alexander Zverevbeats Kevin Anderson 6-7, 7-5, 10-7 to complete a 13-8 win for Team Europe over Team World
Climate activistGreta Thunbergscolds world leaders "How Dare You" for not addressing climate change at the UN Climate Action Summit in New York
Alexander Lukashenkosworn in for sixth term as President of Belarus in a secret ceremony, amid continued widespread protests against his re-election
Kentucky grand jury indicts only one of three officers for wanton engagement for shooting unarmedBreonna Taylorin Louisville
Bidenadministration and EPA introduce first regulation against greenhouse gases, reduction of hydrofluorocarbons by 85% in 15 years
British PMLiz Trussand Chancellor Kwasi Kwarteng release "the mini-budget that broke Britain" containing largest tax cuts. Leads to an economic crisis and the downfall of both.
St. Louis Cardinals sluggerAlbert Pujolshits 2 home runs with 5 RBI in 11-0 win over Dodgers in LA; becomes fourth player in MLB history to hit 700 career HRs
Tennis greatRoger Federerplays his final professional match during Laver Cup in London; teams with friend and rivalRafael Nadalbut loses 6–4, 6–7 [9–11] to Americans Jack Sock and Frances Tiafoe
US PresidentJoe Bidenawards British singerElton Johnwith National Humanities Medal
John Paul Jones aboard the USS Bonhomme Richard defeats the British frigate HMS Serepis and becomes the United States first well-known naval hero
PresidentDwight D. Eisenhowerorders US troops to support the integration of nine Black students at Little Rock Central High School in Arkansas
Geoffrey II, Duke of Brittany and Earl of Richmond through his marriage to Constance, Duchess of Brittany, born in Oxford, England (d. 1186)
Yolande of Valois, Duchess of Savoy, daughter of King Charles VII of France, born in Tours, France (d. 1478)
Louise de Coligny, French 4th wife of William of Orange, born in Châtillon-sur-Loing, France (d. 1620)
Michael Lohr, German composer, born in Marienberg, Saxony (now Germany) (d. 1654)
Joan Blaeu, Dutch cartographer and publisher (1st to use heliocentric theories of Nicolaus Copernicus, Atlas Major), born in Alkmaar (d. 1673)
Eleonora Gonzaga, Holy Roman Empress, German Queen, Queen of Hungary and Bohemia by marriage to Emperor Ferdinand II, born in Mantua, Duchy of Mantua (d. 1655)
Daniel von Czepko, German poet, born in Koszowice, Silesia, (d. 1660)
Adrian van Wijck, Dutch archpriest of Delft who opposed Jansenism, born in Rotterdam, Dutch Republic (d. 1719)[1]
Giovanni Maria Bononcini, Italian violinist, composer, and theorist (Musico prattico), born in Modena, Duchy of Modena and Reggio (d. 1678)
Joseph Dudley, American President of the Dominion of New England and first Chief Justice of the New York Supreme Court of Judicature, born in Roxbury, Massachusetts Bay Colony (d. 1720)[1][2]
Jeremy Collier, British bishop and leader of the Non-jurors (Short View of the Immorality and Profaneness of the English Stage), born in Stow cum Quy, England (d. 1726)[1][2]
Johann Theodor Römhild, German organist and composer, born in Salzungen, Thuringia, Saxony (now Germany) (d. 1756)
Jean-Marie Leclair le cadet (the younger), French composer, born in Lyon, France (d. 1777)
Ferdinand VI, the wise, King of Spain (1746-59), born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1759)
Go-Sakuramachi [Toshiko], 117th Empress of Japan (1762-71) and 8th woman to rule Japan, born in Kyōto, Japan (d. 1813)
John Sevier, American soldier and Governor of Tennessee (1796-1801, 1803-09), born in Augusta County, Virginia (d. 1815)
William Wallace, Scottish mathematician (Rights of Wallace, invented the eidograph), born at Dysart, Scotland (d. 1843)
Kōkaku [Morohito], 119th Emperor of Japan (1779-1817), born in Kyōto, Japan (d. 1840)
Princess Juliane of Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld,  later also known as Grand Duchess Anna Feodorovna of Russia, born in Coburg, Electorate of Bavaria (d. 1860)
Jacques-Fereol Mazas, French violinist, composer, and conductor, born in Lavaur, France (d. 1849)
Johann Franz Encke, German astronomer (Encke Comet), born in Hamburg, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1865)
Theodor Körner, German soldier and poet (Zriny, Leyer und Schwerdt), born in Dresden, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1813)
Alexander Twilight, minister and politician, first African American to graduate and to hold public office, born in Corinth Vermont (d. 1857)
William Holmes McGuffey, American professor (wrote elementary school McGuffey Readers - 120 million copies sold), born in Claysville, Pennsylvania (d. 1873)
David Van de Kellen, Dutch coin engraver, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1879)
Elihu B. Washburne, American politician and diplomat, Minister to France (1869-77), born in Livermore, Massachusetts (d. 1887)
Julius White, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Cazenovia, New York (d. 1890)
Léon Kreutzer, French composer and music critic, born in Paris France (d. 1868)
Armand-Hippolyte-Louis Fizeau, French physicist known for the Fizeau Effect and Doppler Effect, born in Paris, Kingdom of France (d. 1896)[1]
Thomas Kilby Smith, American lawyer, diplomat and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1887)
Karl Munzinger, Swiss conductor and composer, born in Balsthal, Switzerland (d. 1911)
Mikhail Ivanov, Russian composer, born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1927)
William Stewart Halsted, American pioneering surgeon (introduced anesthesia and antisepsis while addicted to cocaine and opium), born in New York City (d. 1922)[1]
Marcel Planiol, French law scholar, born in Nantes, France (d. 1931)
Cornelis Lely, Dutch civil engineer (Zuiderzee dam and dyke works), and politician (Governor of Suriname, 1902-05; Senator, 1910-13), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1929)
William Archer, Scottish critic and playwright (Green Goddess, translated and popularised Ibsen), born in Perth, Scotland (d. 1924)
Robert Bosch, German engineer, inventor (automotive ignition device), and industrialist, born in Albeck, Kingdom of Württemberg (now Germany) (d. 1942)
Alexandre Yersin, Swiss French bacteriologist (discovered bubonic plague bacillus), born in Aubonne, Switzerland (d. 1943)
Mary Church Terrell, American educator and civil rights activist (co-founder and President National Association of Colored Women), born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1954)
Emmuska Orczy, Hungarian-born British Baroness and writer (The Scarlet Pimpernel), born in Tarnaörs Hungary (d. 1947)
Édouard Combe, Swiss composer, born in Aigle, Switzerland (d. 1942)
Draga Mašin, Queen of Serbia and wife of King Alexander I, born in Gornji Milanovac, Serbia (d. 1903)[1]
John Lomax, American folk song collector and ethnomusicologist (American Folklore Society), and educator, born in Goodman, Mississippi (d. 1948)
Frantisek Kupka, Czech pioneering abstract artist, born in Opočno, Austria-Hungary (d. 1957)
John Boyd Orr, Scottish physician (Nobel Peace Prize 1949 - Father of the Food and Agriculture Organization), born in Kilmaurs, Scotland (d. 1971)[1]
Alfieri Maserati, Italian auto racer and engineer (established Maserati Racing), born in Voghera, Italy (d. 1932)
Gerhard Kittel, German anti-semitic theologist (Die Oden Salomons), born in Breslau, Germany (d. 1948)
Arthur Walter Kramer, American composer, born in New York City (d. 1969)
Kornelis Heiko Miskotte, Dutch theologist (Edda & Thora), born in Utrecht, Netherlands (d. 1976)
Miron Merzhanov, Russian architect who designed buildings for Joseph Stalin in the Black Sea region, born in Nakhichevan-on-Don, Russian Empire (d. 1975)[1]
Paul Delvaux, Belgian surrealist painter, born in Wanze, Belgium (d. 1994)
Walter Pidgeon, Canadian actor (Mrs Miniver, Madame Curie), born in Saint John, New Brunswick (d. 1984)
Louise Nevelson, American sculptor who featured at the 31st Venice Biennale (Sky Cathedral), born in Pereiaslav, Ukraine, Russian Empire (d. 1988)
Bill Stone, British serviceman; one of the last surviving veterans of World War I, born in Ledstone, England (d. 2009)
Fred Koch, American chemicalengineerandentrepreneur(Koch Industries), born in Quanah, Texas (d. 1967)
Adriaan Pitlo, Dutch lawyer, born in Antwerp (d. 1987)
Jaroslav Seifert, Czech poet (Nobel Prize for Literature 1984), born in Prague, Austria-Hungary (d. 1986)[1]
Su Buqing, Chinese mathematician and educator, regarded as founder of differential geometry in China, born in Pingyang, China (d. 2003)[1][2]
Meyer Schapiro, Lithuanian-born American art historian, born in Šiauliai, Lithuania, Russian Empire (d. 1996)
Jenö Kenessey, Hungarian conductor and composer, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1976)
Willard McMurry, American blues record label executive (Trumpet Records), born in Lauderdale, Mississippi (d. 1996)
Dominique Aury, French author (The Story of O, Literary Landfalls), born in Rochefort, France (d. 1998)[1]
Duarte Nuno, Duke of Braganza, unfulfilled claimant to the throne of Portugal, born in Seebenstein, Austria (d. 1976)
Herbert Kappler, German head of Nazi police and security services (SS) in Rome during WWII and war criminal responsible for the Ardeatine massacre, born in Stuttgart, German Empire (d. 1978)
Jarmila Novotná, Czech-American soprano (Metropolitan Opera, 1940-56), and actress (The Search), born in Prague, Kingdom of Bohemia (now Czech Republic) (d. 1994)
Marianne Straub,  Swiss-born English textile designer and weaver (Festival of Britain, Piccadilly line seat pattern), born in Amriswil, Switzerland (d. 1994)
Susan Travers, English-French Foreign Legion soldier, born in London, England (d. 2003)
Elliot Roosevelt, American general, writer and son of FDR (Murder in the Oval Office), born in New York City (d. 1990)
Soulima Stravinsky, Swiss-American pianist, composer, and son of Igor Stravinsky, born in Lausanne, Switzerland (d. 1994)
Frank Moss, American Democratic politician (Senator Utah 1959-77), born in Salt Lake City, Utah (d. 2003)[1]
Ghulam Mustafa Khan, Pakistani researcher, critic and linguist specializing in Urdu, born in Jabalpur, India (d. 2005)
Tony Smith, American sculptor and painter (Minimalist art movement), born in South Orange, New Jersey (d. 1980)
Aleksi Machavariani, Soviet Georgian conductor, and composer (Den moei Rodini (Day of My Motherland); Five Monologues), born in Gori, Georgia (d. 1995)
Norman Cazden, American composer, educator, and music theorist, born in New York City (d. 1980)
Omar Ali Saifuddien III, 28th Sultan of Brunei (1950-67), born in Istana Kota, Brunei (d. 1986)
Finn Arnestad, Norwegian contemporary classical composer (Ritagliata), born in Oslo, Norway (d. 1994)
Ian Dawson-Shepherd, British campaigner for cerebrally palsied people, born in Port Said, Egypt (d. 1996)
Anna Yegorova, Russian pilot (flew 277 sorties WWII in Soviet air force), born in Volodovo, Russia (d. 2009)
Imry Németh, Hungarian athlete (Olympic gold hammer throw 1948, bronze 1952; WR 1959 59.88m), born in Košice, Slovakia (d. 1989)
Alexander Arutiunian [Harut'unyan], Soviet Armenian classical pianist and composer (Trumpet Concerto), born in Yerevan, First Republic of Armenia (d. 2012)
Gerard W. Taylor, South African-British surgeon, born in Natal, South Africa (d. 1995)
Jiri Jaroch, Czech composer, born in Smilkov (d. 1986)
Philip Sherrard, English author and translator of modern Greek, born in Oxford, England (d. 1995)
Samuel V. Wilson, American ex-director of the Defense Intelligence Agency and former Army lieutenant general, born in Rice, Virginia (d. 2017)
Barry Linehan, English actor (Bullseye, Dark Places, Witchcraft), born in Romford, England (d. 1996)
Denis Twitchett, British historian and co-editor of the Cambridge History of China, born in London (d. 2006)
Eleonora Rossi Drago [Palmira Omiccioli], Italian actress (Le amiche), born in Quinto al Mare, Genoa (d. 2007)
Henry Silva, American character actor (Ocean's 11; The Manchurian Candidate; Sharky's Machine), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2022)
Frank Foster, American jazz saxophonist, arranger, composer, band leader (Count Basie; The Loud Minority), and educator, born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 2011)
Robert Helps, American pianist, prodigy, pedagogue, and composer, born in Passaic, New Jersey (d. 2001)
Wally Whyton, English musician and broadcaster (BBC 2), born in London, England (d. 1997)
Colin Blakely, Northern-Irish character actor (Shattered, Equus, King Lear), born in Bangor, County Down (d. 1987)
Don Edmunds, American auto racer and car builder (National Sprint Car HOF; built Evel Knievel's Snake River Canyon Sky cycle), born in Santa Ana, California (d. 2020)
Irene Reid, American jazz singer ("I Must Be Doing Something Right"), born in Savannah, Georgia (d. 2008)
Charles Harrison, American industrial designer (view finder), born in Shreveport, Louisiana (d. 2018)
Gerald Stairs Merrithew, Canadian high school principal and Progressive Conservative politician (MP 1984-93), born in Saint John, New Brunswick (d. 2004)[1][2]
Sergio Brighenti, Italian soccer forward (9 caps; Modena, Internazionale, Triestina, Padova, Sampdoria), born in Modena, Italy (d. 2022)
